structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: This preface section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document provides an overview of its structure and evolution. It introduces key concepts such as the document's identification, status, and changes across different AUTOSAR releases. The document is part of the Classic Platform standard release R25-11 and details revisions including updates to error codes, transformer parameters, and API definitions. Relationships between different sections are outlined, with specific attention given to error handling, APIs, and configuration specifications. Important technical details include changes in reentrancy status for initialization functions and modifications to supported configuration variants.

structure.title: 1 Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document introduces the fundamental concepts and architecture of transformers in AUTOSAR systems. It defines a transformer as a component that enables data transformation for linearization and modification, which can be chained together to form transformer chains for intra-ECU and inter-ECU communication. Each transformer has well-defined function signatures based on the transmitted data elements, ensuring consistent processing. The output of each transformer is always a linear byte array, allowing flexible addition of functionalities such as safety or security protection through chaining multiple transformers.

structure.title: 2 Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the acronyms and abbreviations pertinent to the text. It specifies that no additional acronyms or abbreviations are introduced in this particular document beyond those already defined in the AUTOSAR glossary, which is an external reference source. While there are no specific tables provided within this section, it emphasizes the importance of adhering to a standardized terminology as maintained by the AUTOSAR organization to ensure consistency and clarity throughout the technical specification.

structure.title: 4 Constraints and assumptions
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the constraints and assumptions that underpin the design and implementation of transformer models. The summary highlights important relationships between these constraints and the operational processes of transformers, emphasizing how they impact performance and scalability. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, readers should be aware that detailed technical specifications and limitations are likely presented elsewhere in the document to guide practical applications of transformer models.

nodes.title: 4.1 Limitations
nodes.summary: This section discusses the limitations and assumptions imposed on transformers in the context of data transformation and communication. It introduces key concepts such as VariableDataPrototypes, ClientServerOperations, TriggerInterfaces, and ISignals, which are subject to transformation. The document outlines constraints related to PDU sizes and the nature of transformations, noting that while multiple transformed data elements can be aggregated into a single PDU within Com, they must be processed independently. Additionally, it specifies an artificial limit on the length of transformer chains due to memory efficiency considerations, with a maximum chain length set at 255.

nodes.title: 4.2 Applicability to car domains
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document discusses the applicability of transformers in automotive domains without any restrictions. It introduces key concepts related to how transformer models can be effectively utilized in various car-related applications, such as autonomous driving and vehicle diagnostics. The text highlights that there are no limitations on using these models within the automotive sector, suggesting a broad range of potential uses. While specific technical details or constraints from tables are not provided in this excerpt, it sets the stage for further exploration into the practical implementation and benefits of applying transformer architectures in car-related technologies.

structure.title: 5 Dependencies to other modules
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document discusses the dependencies between transformers and other modules within a system. It highlights that there are no dependencies on AUTOSAR Software Components (SWS) modules, indicating an independent design for the transformer module in question. This independence suggests that the transformer can operate without relying on specific SWS components, potentially simplifying integration or reducing external dependencies. While no tables are present in this section, it emphasizes the modular and self-contained nature of the transformer's design.

nodes.title: 5.1 File structure
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the file structure for transformer models, detailing how various components and data are organized within these files. It introduces key concepts such as model parameters, configuration settings, and input/output formats. The summary explains that each file is structured to maintain a clear separation between different aspects of the model, including weights, hyperparameters, and metadata. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it emphasizes the importance of adhering to a standardized structure for compatibility across different systems and applications.

nodes.title: 5.1.1 Code file structure
nodes.summary: This section outlines the code file structure for transformers, which are categorized as BSW (Software and System Work Breakdown) modules according to the [2, SWS BSW General] standard. Deviations from this structure are documented in specific transformer SWS documents. The classification of transformers as BSW modules establishes a standardized approach to their development and management, ensuring consistency across different projects while allowing for flexibility through specified deviations.

nodes.title: 5.1.2 Header file structure
nodes.summary: This section outlines the structure of header files for transformers, which are classified as BSW (Body Side Module) modules according to [2, SWS BSW General]. Key components include the specific definitions and deviations detailed in the SWS documents related to individual transformers. The classification as BSW modules implies certain standardized processes and constraints that these header files must adhere to, with room for customization through documented exceptions.

structure.title: 6 Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on tracing requirements to ensure comprehensive coverage and compliance. It introduces key concepts such as SRS (System Requirements Specification) and BSW (Basic Software Modules), detailing how specific transformer functionalities align with these requirements. The document outlines important relationships between different transformer classes, error handling mechanisms, and data processing capabilities. Through a detailed table, it maps out which sections of the document satisfy various SRS requirements, ensuring that all necessary aspects are addressed in the implementation.

structure.title: 7 Functional Specification
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the functional requirements and specifications for transformers within the Communication Service Cluster. It introduces key concepts such as stateful vs. stateless transformers, transformer chains, and their execution order. The document emphasizes that transformers must define output formats, endianness, data semantics, and padding to ensure correct processing by target systems. Important relationships include the coordination between the RTE and transformers for both intra-ECU and inter-ECU communication, as well as optimization techniques for reducing redundant transformations in scenarios with signal fanouts. Technical details cover the handling of unexpected input data, reentrancy requirements, and specific configurations for accessing original data during transformer chain execution.

nodes.title: 7.1 Buffer Handling
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the buffer handling mechanisms used by transformers in data processing. It introduces two modes of buffer handling: in-place and out-of-place buffering. Key concepts include the transformer's interface, which is influenced by its buffer mode, and the role of the Rte (likely a resource or runtime environment) in allocating buffers based on worst-case output size calculations. The document specifies that transformers cannot modify input data when using in-place buffering and that certain constraints apply to where in-place buffering can be used within the transformer chain. Additionally, it mandates out-of-place buffering for the first sending-side and last receiving-side transformers due to specific context requirements.

nodes.title: 7.2 Transformer Classes
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document discusses the categorization and usage of transformers in different classes based on their functionality. It introduces key transformer classes such as Serializer, Safety, Security, and Custom, emphasizing that each class contains transformers with similar functions. The specification also outlines a constraint limiting the inclusion of at most one transformer from each class within a single transformer chain. Additionally, it notes that future AUTOSAR releases may define further transformer classes, indicating an evolving standard in this technical area.

nodes.title: 7.2.1 Serializer
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on the Serializer transformer's role in converting complex data elements into a byte array for transmission. Key concepts include the serialization process and its requirements as defined by upstream specifications SRS_Xfrm_00001, with specific constraints on supported data types. The serializer must handle both complex and atomic data inputs, ensuring all possible complex data are covered by the serialization algorithm. Additionally, it highlights that only "new-world" variable-size array data types can be processed, referencing external documentation for further details.

nodes.title: 7.2.2 Safety
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on the safety aspects of transformers used in inter-ECU (Electronic Control Unit) communication. Key concepts include ensuring safe data transmission by protecting against unintentional modifications, maintaining the correct order and content of data transmissions. The document specifies that these protections can be achieved through mechanisms such as adding sequence counters and checksums to meet safety requirements. These specifications are derived from upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00001, emphasizing the importance of robust communication protocols in ensuring reliable and secure data exchange between ECUs.

nodes.title: 7.2.3 Security
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on security measures for transformers used in inter-ECU (Electronic Control Unit) communication. Key concepts include ensuring the authenticity, integrity, and freshness of data transmissions to protect against intentional modifications. The security transformer must authenticate the source of data and verify that it has not been altered or become stale during transmission. These requirements are derived from upstream specification SRS_Xfrm_00001. To meet these security goals, sequence counters and checksums can be employed as technical solutions.

nodes.title: 7.2.4 Custom
nodes.summary: This section discusses custom transformers in the context of the General Specification of Transformers document. It explains that while AUTOSAR does not specify these transformers, they can be defined by any party involved in the development process to address non-standard requirements. Custom transformers are mentioned as being implementable through Component Definition Descriptions (CDDs). The section highlights the flexibility and extensibility provided by allowing custom implementations but does not include specific technical details from tables.

nodes.title: 7.3 Error Handling
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers" focuses on the error handling mechanism for transformers within a system. It introduces key concepts such as soft and hard errors, with specific return code ranges (0x01-0x7F for soft errors and 0x80-0xFF for hard errors). The document outlines that transformers should return errors to the Real-Time Executive (RTE), which decides whether to continue or abort the execution of the transformer chain based on these error codes. It also specifies that if a transformer encounters an issue generating valid output, it must return a hard error and leave the output buffer unchanged. Additionally, each transformer class has its own set of abstract errors, and transformers can only return errors defined within their respective classes. The summary notes that soft errors in early stages might be masked by subsequent hard errors, highlighting the importance of proper handling and evaluation of these errors at higher levels.

nodes.title: 7.3.1 Errors of Serializer Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on the errors associated with serializer transformers. It introduces key error codes and their classifications (soft vs hard) along with detailed descriptions for each error type. The document outlines specific conditions under which these errors occur, such as when there is no data available for deserialization or when the payload length exceeds expectations. Additionally, it reserves certain error codes to ensure compliance with SOME/IP standards and to avoid conflicts in autonomous error handling.

nodes.title: 7.3.2 Errors of Safety Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on errors associated with safety transformers. It introduces error codes and their classifications, detailing scenarios where data transmission is safe versus unsafe based on various conditions such as repeated counters, counter jumps, check failures, and lack of new data. The document categorizes these errors into soft and hard types, providing descriptions that explain the nature of each error. Key components include specific error names like `E_OK` for successful communication and `E_SAFETY_INVALID_NND` for invalid communications with no new data received, along with their corresponding hexadecimal codes. Relationships between different error conditions are clearly outlined, emphasizing how certain errors can lead to others or affect the state machine's operation.

nodes.title: 7.3.3 Errors of Security Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on errors associated with security transformers. It introduces key error codes and their corresponding descriptions, which are essential for understanding potential issues in secure data communication. Specifically, it defines three error types: `E_OK`, indicating a successful secure communication; `E_SEC_NOT_AUTH`, signifying that the data was not authenticated correctly, classified as a soft error; and `E_SEC_NOT_FRESH`, denoting that the data was not fresh, also categorized as a soft error. These definitions are directly linked to upstream requirements (SRS_Xfrm_00010) and provide critical insights into the security validation processes of transformers.

nodes.title: 7.3.4 Errors of Custom Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on errors associated with custom transformers. It introduces an error coding system to categorize and identify different types of errors that can occur during their operation. Specifically, it defines three categories of errors: no error (E_OK), soft errors (0x01-0x7F), and hard errors (0x80-0xFF). The section establishes a clear relationship between the error codes and their corresponding error types, providing a structured approach to handling and diagnosing issues in custom transformer implementations.

nodes.title: 7.4 Error Classification
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on error classification within transformer models. It introduces key concepts such as different types of errors and their classifications based on specific criteria like model mispredictions during training. The section outlines important relationships between various error categories and their impact on overall model performance, emphasizing constraints related to error handling and mitigation strategies. Although no tables are explicitly mentioned in the provided content, it likely includes detailed definitions and examples that help in understanding how errors are categorized and managed within transformer architectures.

nodes.title: 7.4.1 Development Errors
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on defining development errors within the transformer module. It introduces key error codes and their corresponding values for various types of errors that can occur during the development process. Specifically, it outlines error conditions such as calling an API service before or after initialization/deinitialization, selecting invalid configurations, passing incorrect parameters, and using invalid pointers. The document also specifies that MIP (Module Implementation Prefix) is used to denote these error codes, which are defined in [SWS_BSW_00102].

nodes.title: 7.4.2 Runtime Errors
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document discusses runtime errors in transformer models. It explicitly states that there are no runtime errors encountered during the operation of these models. While it does not introduce any new key concepts, definitions, or components, it implies a robust error handling mechanism is in place to prevent such issues from arising. The absence of runtime errors suggests a high level of reliability and stability in the transformer model's execution environment.

nodes.title: 7.4.3 Production Errors
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document addresses production errors in transformer manufacturing. It explicitly states that there are no reported production errors, indicating a high level of quality control and adherence to manufacturing standards throughout the process. While it does not introduce any new key concepts or definitions, this statement is significant as it implies that all transformers meet the specified quality criteria without encountering any defects during production. The absence of production errors suggests a well-managed and reliable manufacturing process, ensuring consistent performance and reliability of the transformers.

nodes.title: 7.4.4 Extended Production Errors
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on Extended Production Errors for transformers. It outlines specific error conditions that can arise during the manufacturing process and provides detailed specifications for identifying and addressing these issues. The chapter likely includes definitions of various production errors, their causes, and potential impacts on transformer performance. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, such technical details might be presented to guide engineers in diagnosing and mitigating these errors effectively.

nodes.title: 7.4.4.1 XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the error condition `XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE`, which occurs when a transformer fails to produce output due to malformed input data. Key concepts include the detection criteria for this error, where it is triggered by non-conformant input formats relative to the transformer's specifications. The section also specifies that this error must be implemented for any transformer configured with `XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE`. Importantly, the document details that this error should be monitored on every execution of the transformer, ensuring continuous validation of input data integrity.

nodes.title: 7.5 Error Notification
nodes.summary: The section "7.5 Error Notification" in the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the mechanisms and protocols for error reporting within transformer systems as defined in [2, SWS BSW General]. It introduces key concepts such as error codes, notification channels, and handling procedures to ensure reliable communication of faults or malfunctions. The section describes how errors are categorized and reported through specific channels, emphasizing the importance of timely and accurate error notifications for system maintenance and operation. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, it is implied that detailed error code definitions and corresponding actions might be provided elsewhere in the document.

structure.title: 8 API specification
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on the API specification for transformers, detailing how these models can be interfaced with external systems. It introduces key concepts such as input and output formats, supported operations, and error handling mechanisms. The summary outlines important relationships between different API components and constraints related to data types and response times. Additionally, it includes relevant technical details from accompanying tables that specify the exact parameter requirements for various API calls and their expected return values.

nodes.title: 8.1 Imported types
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the requirements for imported data types in transformer modules. It specifies that transformers must use certain types defined by Real-Time Environment (RTE) and other standard types as listed in a table. The key components include `Rte_Cs_TransactionHandleType`, various `Std_Types.h` types like `Std_ExtractProtocolHeaderFieldsType`, and others, all of which are essential for module interlinking. Importantly, the use of these types is constrained by upstream requirements and defined in specific header files, ensuring consistency across different modules and components within the system.

nodes.title: 8.2 Type definitions
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document defines a specific data type, `{Mip}_ConfigType`, which is crucial for initializing transformers. It is structured and defined in the header file `<Mip>.h`. The initialization data for the transformer is encapsulated within this structure, though its exact elements are implementation-specific, meaning they can vary based on the particular application or design requirements. This type definition is closely tied to upstream requirements SRS_BSW_00404 and SRS_BSW_00441, indicating that these specifications provide the necessary context and constraints for its use in transformer initialization processes.

nodes.title: 8.3 Function definitions
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the naming conventions and function signatures for transformer APIs. It introduces key concepts such as `transformerId`, which is derived based on specific patterns related to communication signals, signal groups, or IPDU names. The pattern also considers the context provided by component, port, and variable data prototypes. Importantly, it describes how the presence of a `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef` influences both the naming and generation of transformer functions, potentially leading to multiple functions for a single signal if referenced in multiple mappings. These definitions enhance the clarity and manageability of the API design while ensuring that each function is uniquely identifiable within its context.

nodes.title: 8.3.1 <Mip>_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields
nodes.summary: This section describes the API function `<Mip>_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields`, which is crucial for extracting relevant protocol header fields and message result types in a transformer. The function takes a buffer and its length as input parameters and returns the extracted message type and result type in canonical form through output arguments. It operates synchronously and is reentrant, meaning it can be safely called multiple times without issues. The section outlines that this function should return `E_OK` upon successful extraction or `E_NOT_OK` if an error occurs during parsing, ensuring robust handling of protocol headers in transformer processes.

nodes.title: 8.3.2 <Mip>_<transformerId>
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the API functions for transformers used in various communication contexts such as Sender/Receiver and Client/Server communications. Key concepts include the `Mip` (Module Implementation Prefix) and `transformerId`, which define the function names. The document describes multiple transformer functions, each with specific parameters and return values, and their roles within a transformer chain. Important relationships are established between these functions and the corresponding ISignals or DataTransformations they serve. Constraints include the reentrancy of some functions and the handling of error codes. Technical details specify the service names, parameter types, buffer management, and synchronization aspects for each function.

nodes.title: 8.3.3 <Mip>_Inv_<transformerId>
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the API function `<Mip>_Inv_<transformerId>` for various types of communication (Sender/Receiver, Client/Server, and external trigger events). Key concepts include synchronous and reentrant functions that handle data transformation. The function takes a buffer as input and outputs transformed data or operation arguments. Important relationships are defined between the transformer's position in the chain and its role in different communication contexts, with constraints on when certain parameters can be NULL or zero-length. Technical details specify the return values for success or error conditions, and note that variable size arrays may require updates based on deserialization.

nodes.title: 8.3.4 <Mip>_Init
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the initialization process for a transformer module via the API function `<Mip>_Init`. Key concepts include the synchronous nature of the function and its non-reentrant property, which means it cannot be called recursively. The function takes a pointer to configuration data as an input parameter but does not return any values or modify output parameters. It is defined in the header file `<Mip>.h` and is subject to specific upstream requirements such as SRS_BSW_00407 and SRS_BSW_00411, ensuring its implementation adheres to certain standards. The initialization process prepares the transformer for further processing by applying the provided configuration settings.

nodes.title: 8.3.5 <Mip> _DeInit
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the API function `<Mip>_DeInit`, which deinitializes a transformer. Key concepts include its synchronous nature and non-reentrant property, with no parameters or return values. The function is defined in the header file `<Mip>.h` and adheres to specific upstream requirements (SRS_BSW_00407, SRS_BSW_00411, and SRS_Xfrm_00002). The module implementation prefix `Mip` is derived from [SWS_BSW_00102], ensuring consistency across the transformer's implementation.

nodes.title: 8.3.6 <Mip>_GetVersionInfo
nodes.summary: This section describes the API function `<Mip>_GetVersionInfo`, which retrieves version information for a transformer module. It introduces key elements such as the service name and syntax, parameters, return value, and availability through the `<Mip>.h` header file. The function is synchronous and reentrant, with no input or output parameters other than the `VersionInfo` pointer used to store the retrieved version information. This function adheres to specific upstream requirements and follows defined rules for its variable prototype parts, ensuring consistency across implementations.

nodes.title: 8.4 Callback notifications
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document discusses callback notifications within the context of transformer operations. It explicitly states that there are no callback notifications implemented, indicating a design choice where such mechanisms are not utilized in the current specification. This absence has implications for how certain events or states are handled and communicated during the operation of transformers. While no tables are present to provide additional technical details, this section sets expectations for developers or users regarding the lack of callback functionality, which could influence their approach to event handling or state management in transformer implementations.

nodes.title: 8.5 Scheduled functions
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document discusses scheduled functions for transformers. It highlights that there are no universal scheduled functions applicable to all types of transformers, indicating a need for specific scheduling strategies tailored to individual transformer models or applications. While this excerpt does not introduce any new key concepts, definitions, or components, it sets an important context by emphasizing the variability in scheduling requirements across different transformer designs and uses. structure.title: 10 Configuration specification
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the configuration parameters and their organization into containers for transformers. It introduces key concepts such as system-level and software component-level configurations, which are essential for generating a basic ECU (Electronic Control Unit) model. The structure and parameters of transformers are detailed in Section 10.2, while foundational information is provided in Chapter 10.1 to ensure clarity. A template for parameter specification is also specified, though its inclusion is intended to enhance understanding rather than be strictly adhered to.

nodes.summary: This section provides guidance on how to interpret and understand the content of Chapter 10.1 "Introduction to configuration specification" within the document "General Specification of Transformers." nodes.title: 10.2 Containers and configuration parameters
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the configuration parameters and containers used in transformer configurations within an AUTOSAR framework. It introduces key concepts such as `EcucModuleDef Xfrm`, which defines the module's description, post-build variant support, and supported configuration variants. The document also details two important containers: `XfrmGeneral` for general configuration parameters and `XfrmImplementationMapping` for specifying mappings between transformers and BswModuleEntries in a transformer chain. These mappings are crucial for implementing transformations on ISignals, ensuring that the correct implementation is applied based on the specified variant.

nodes.title: 10.2.1 XfrmGeneral
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the configuration parameters for the `XfrmGeneral` container. It introduces key parameters such as `XfrmDevErrorDetect`, `XfrmInstanceId`, and `XfrmVersionInfoApi`, each with specific roles in module configuration. The relationships between these parameters and their parent container are clearly defined, along with constraints on their values and default settings. Notably, the document specifies that `XfrmInstanceId` must be set to 0 if only one instance is present, and it details how these parameters can be configured at different stages of development.

nodes.title: 10.2.2 XfrmImplementationMapping
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document describes the `XfrmImplementationMapping` container and its key parameters for mapping transformers to specific BSW module entries in a data transformation chain. It introduces concepts such as `XfrmTransformerClassExtractProtocolHeaderFields`, `XfrmCSTransactionHandleImplementationDataTypeRef`, and `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef`, which are used to configure how transformers handle protocol headers, transaction handles, and variable data prototypes respectively. The section outlines important relationships and constraints, particularly regarding the use of `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef` for intra-ECU communication or when multiple receivers require specific transformer functions for end-to-end (E2E) protected communication. Additionally, it specifies that if no ISignal is referenced, `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef` must be used to reference the VariableDataPrototype instance for transformation purposes.

nodes.title: 10.2.3 XfrmSignal
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document outlines the configuration and reference mechanisms for `XfrmSignal`, which defines how transformed data signals are referenced in a system description. Key components include `XfrmSignalChoice` for selecting between referencing an individual signal (`ISignal`) or a group of signals (`ISignalGroup`). The section details the multiplicity, dependencies, and configuration parameters for these choices, emphasizing that only one reference can be made at any given time. Importantly, it describes how foreign references to `ISignalGroup` and `ISignal` are established through specific parameter definitions, highlighting the pre-compile nature of their values and the constraints on variant configurations.

nodes.title: 10.2.4 XfrmDemEventParameterRefs
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers" document focuses on defining and describing key elements related to error handling in transformer implementations. It introduces the `XfrmDemEventParameterRefs` container, which holds references to specific `DemEventParameter` elements that should be invoked via the API `Dem_SetEventStatus`. The document outlines the multiplicity constraints for these parameters and provides detailed definitions of included parameters like `XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE`, which refers to configured DEM events for reporting malformed messages. Additionally, it references several meta-classes such as `ArrayImplPolicyEnum` and `SwComponentPrototype`, illustrating how these components interact within the broader context of AUTOSAR software architecture. The relationships between error handling mechanisms and API functions are crucial, with specific constraints and definitions provided to ensure proper behavior during runtime.
